# test-fsemu-cases.txt -- FS-UAE test suite for jot 1.56
# jot: print sequential or random data
# Category: 1 (CLI utility)
# Generated by test-designer agent

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Basic sequences
# ============================================================

# Native: jot 5 | head -1 => "1"; jot 5 | tail -1 => "5"
TEST: Basic sequence jot 5 prints 1 through 5
CMD: WORK:jot 5
EXPECT: 1
EXPECT_LINE: 5,5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot 5 1 5 | head -1 => "1"; | tail -1 => "5"
TEST: Explicit begin=1 end=5 with 5 reps is same as jot 5
CMD: WORK:jot 5 1 5
EXPECT: 1
EXPECT_LINE: 5,5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot 3 10 | tr '\n' '|' => "10|11|12|"
TEST: Begin at 10 produces 10 11 12 when reps=3
CMD: WORK:jot 3 10
EXPECT: 10
EXPECT_LINE: 3,12
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Descending sequences
# ============================================================

# Native: jot 5 10 1 => 10,8,6,3,1 (step = -2.25, prec=0 rounds each value)
TEST: Descending sequence from 10 to 1 with 5 reps
CMD: WORK:jot 5 10 1
EXPECT: 10
EXPECT_LINE: 2,8
EXPECT_LINE: 3,6
EXPECT_LINE: 4,3
EXPECT_LINE: 5,1
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Floating point sequences
# ============================================================

# Native: jot 5 0.0 1.0 => 0.0,0.2,0.5,0.8,1.0
# step = (1.0-0.0)/(5-1) = 0.25, but prec=1 from "0.0" arg -> rounds to 1dp
TEST: Float sequence 0.0 to 1.0 uses prec=1 from argument
CMD: WORK:jot 5 0.0 1.0
EXPECT: 0.0
EXPECT_LINE: 2,0.3
EXPECT_LINE: 3,0.5
EXPECT_LINE: 5,1.0
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot -p 2 3 1 2 => 1.00,1.50,2.00
TEST: Flag -p 2 forces two decimal places
CMD: WORK:jot -p 2 3 1 2
EXPECT: 1.00
EXPECT_LINE: 2,1.50
EXPECT_LINE: 3,2.00
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot 3 0 1 => 0,0,1 (prec=0, step=0.5 rounds to int)
TEST: Float step 0.5 with prec=0 rounds intermediate values
CMD: WORK:jot 3 0 1
EXPECT: 0
EXPECT_LINE: 3,1
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Step size
# ============================================================

# Native: jot 5 1 9 2 => 1,3,5,7,9 (explicit step=2)
TEST: Explicit step=2 produces odd numbers 1 3 5 7 9
CMD: WORK:jot 5 1 9 2
EXPECT: 1
EXPECT_LINE: 3,5
EXPECT_LINE: 5,9
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Flag -b (boring/word repeat)
# ============================================================

# Native: jot -b hello 3 => hello,hello,hello
TEST: Flag -b repeats word N times
CMD: WORK:jot -b hello 3
EXPECT: hello
EXPECT_LINE: 3,hello
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Flag -w (format word)
# ============================================================

# Native: jot -w "line %d" 3 => "line 1","line 2","line 3"
TEST: Flag -w with percent-d inserts line number
CMD: WORK:jot -w "line %d" 3
EXPECT: line 1
EXPECT_LINE: 3,line 3
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot -w %03d 5 => 001,002,003,004,005
TEST: Flag -w with zero-padded format produces 001 through 005
CMD: WORK:jot -w %03d 5
EXPECT: 001
EXPECT_LINE: 5,005
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot -w %x 5 10 => a,b,c,d,e  (10-14 hex)
TEST: Flag -w with %x format produces hex output
CMD: WORK:jot -w %x 5 10
EXPECT: a
EXPECT_LINE: 5,e
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot -w %.1f 3 1 3 => 1.0,2.0,3.0
TEST: Flag -w with float format specifier
CMD: WORK:jot -w %.1f 3 1 3
EXPECT: 1.0
EXPECT_LINE: 3,3.0
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Flag -s (separator)
# ============================================================

# Native: jot -s , 5 => "1,2,3,4,5" (single line with comma separator)
TEST: Flag -s comma produces comma-separated single-line output
CMD: WORK:jot -s , 5
EXPECT: 1,2,3,4,5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot -s " " 3 => "1 2 3" (space separator)
TEST: Flag -s space produces space-separated output
CMD: WORK:jot -s " " 3
EXPECT: 1 2 3
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Flag -c (character output)
# ============================================================

# Native: jot -c 5 65 => A,B,C,D,E  (ASCII 65=A)
TEST: Flag -c outputs ASCII characters starting at 65 (A)
CMD: WORK:jot -c 5 65
EXPECT: A
EXPECT_LINE: 5,E
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot -c 5 65 90 => A,G,M,S,Z  (step=6.25)
TEST: Flag -c with begin and end distributes across range
CMD: WORK:jot -c 5 65 90
EXPECT: A
EXPECT_LINE: 3,M
EXPECT_LINE: 5,Z
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Flag -r (random output)
# ============================================================

# -r output is non-deterministic so we only check line count and RC
TEST: Flag -r produces exactly 5 output lines
CMD: WORK:jot -r 5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot -r 5 1 10 produces 5 integers in range 1-10
TEST: Flag -r with integer range produces integer output
CMD: WORK:jot -r 5 1 10
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot -r -p 2 5 1.0 2.0 produces 5 floats with 2 decimal places
TEST: Flag -r with -p 2 produces float output
CMD: WORK:jot -r -p 2 5 1.0 2.0
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Flag -n (no final newline)
# ============================================================

# -n suppresses the trailing newline -- RC should be 0, output truncated
TEST: Flag -n suppresses final newline (RC=0 verified)
CMD: WORK:jot -n 3
EXPECT: 1
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# FUNCTIONAL TESTS -- Combined flags
# ============================================================

# Native: jot -w num%d -s , 3 => "num1,num2,num3"
TEST: Combined -w and -s flags produce formatted comma-separated list
CMD: WORK:jot -w "num%d" -s , 3
EXPECT: num1,num2,num3
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# EDGE CASE TESTS
# ============================================================

# Native: jot 1 => just "1"
TEST: Single repetition produces exactly one line
CMD: WORK:jot 1
EXPECT: 1
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot 3 5 5 => 5,5,5 (begin==end, step=0)
TEST: Begin equals end repeats same value N times
CMD: WORK:jot 3 5 5
EXPECT: 5
EXPECT_LINE: 3,5
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Native: jot 5 -3 3 => -3,-2,0,2,3 (step=1.5, prec=0)
TEST: Negative begin with positive end spans zero
CMD: WORK:jot 5 -3 3
EXPECT: -3
EXPECT_LINE: 3,0
EXPECT_LINE: 5,3
EXPECT_RC: 0

# NOTE: jot -b x 0 enters infinite loop (reps==0 sets inf_loop=true in source).
# This is the intended OpenBSD behavior -- 0 means infinite, not "zero reps".
# Skipped: would hang the test harness.

# ============================================================
# ERROR PATH TESTS
# ============================================================

# Native: jot (no args) => usage message to stderr, exit 1 (Amiga: exit 10)
# Note: error message goes to stderr -- test harness only captures stdout
TEST: No arguments prints usage and exits with RC=10
CMD: WORK:jot -Z
EXPECT_RC: 10

# Native: jot 1 2 3 4 5 => "Too many arguments. What do you mean by 5?"
TEST: Too many arguments exits with RC=10
CMD: WORK:jot 1 2 3 4 5
EXPECT_RC: 10

# Native: jot abc => "Bad reps value: abc"
TEST: Non-numeric reps argument exits with RC=10
CMD: WORK:jot abc
EXPECT_RC: 10

# Native: jot 5 1 10 -1 => "Impossible stepsize"
TEST: Impossible stepsize exits with RC=10
CMD: WORK:jot 5 1 10 -1
EXPECT_RC: 10

# Native: jot -w "%d%d" 3 => "too many conversions"
TEST: Too many format conversions in -w exits with RC=10
CMD: WORK:jot -w "%d%d" 3
EXPECT_RC: 10

# Native: jot -r -p 10 5 => "requested precision too large"
TEST: Random with precision >9 exits with RC=10
CMD: WORK:jot -r -p 10 5
EXPECT_RC: 10

# Bad precision value
TEST: Non-numeric precision argument exits with RC=10
CMD: WORK:jot -p xyz 3
EXPECT_RC: 10

# ============================================================
# REAL-WORLD TESTS
# ============================================================

# Real-world: generate 100-line sequence (tests buffer handling, loop stability)
# Native: jot 100 | tail -1 => "100"
TEST: Generates 100-line sequence correctly (last line is 100)
CMD: WORK:jot 100
EXPECT: 1
EXPECT_LINE: 100,100
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Real-world: generate sequence for use as array indices (1-based)
# Native: jot -w "item%03d" 5 => item001..item005
TEST: Zero-padded items for array index generation
CMD: WORK:jot -w "item%03d" 5
EXPECT: item001
EXPECT_LINE: 5,item005
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Real-world: octal output
# Native: jot -w %o 8 => 1,2,3,4,5,6,7,10
TEST: Octal format for base-8 sequences
CMD: WORK:jot -w %o 8
EXPECT: 1
EXPECT_LINE: 8,10
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Real-world: countdown sequence (descending integers for scripts)
# Native: jot 5 5 1 => 5,4,3,2,1 (step = (1-5)/(5-1) = -1)
TEST: Countdown 5 to 1 for shell script loops
CMD: WORK:jot 5 5 1
EXPECT: 5
EXPECT_LINE: 5,1
EXPECT_RC: 0

# ============================================================
# STRESS / PRECISION TESTS
# ============================================================

# Stress: generate 1000-line sequence
# Native: jot 1000 | tail -1 => "1000"
TEST: Large 1000-line sequence completes correctly
CMD: WORK:jot 1000
EXPECT: 1
EXPECT_LINE: 1000,1000
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Precision: floating point arithmetic must produce mathematically correct values
# Native: jot -p 3 5 0.000 1.000 => 0.000,0.250,0.500,0.750,1.000
# step = 1.0/4 = 0.25 exactly representable in IEEE 754
TEST: Float sequence 0 to 1 with prec=3 and 5 reps is exact
CMD: WORK:jot -p 3 5 0.000 1.000
EXPECT: 0.000
EXPECT_LINE: 2,0.250
EXPECT_LINE: 3,0.500
EXPECT_LINE: 4,0.750
EXPECT_LINE: 5,1.000
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Precision: verify 68k floating point step accumulation for descending float
# Native: jot -p 1 5 2.0 0.0 => 2.0,1.5,1.0,0.5,0.0
# step = (0.0-2.0)/(5-1) = -0.5 exactly representable
TEST: Descending float sequence with exact IEEE 754 step
CMD: WORK:jot -p 1 5 2.0 0.0
EXPECT: 2.0
EXPECT_LINE: 2,1.5
EXPECT_LINE: 3,1.0
EXPECT_LINE: 4,0.5
EXPECT_LINE: 5,0.0
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Stress: high-precision output with -p to exercise printf path
# Native: jot -p 6 3 1 3 => 1.000000,2.000000,3.000000
TEST: High precision -p 6 on exact integers
CMD: WORK:jot -p 6 3 1 3
EXPECT: 1.000000
EXPECT_LINE: 2,2.000000
EXPECT_LINE: 3,3.000000
EXPECT_RC: 0

# Stress: long -b string output (exercises boring/word path)
# Native: jot -b "AMIGAPORT" 10 | tail -1 => "AMIGAPORT"
TEST: Large -b repeat of 10 times produces correct last line
CMD: WORK:jot -b AMIGAPORT 10
EXPECT: AMIGAPORT
EXPECT_LINE: 10,AMIGAPORT
EXPECT_RC: 0
